pattern: A trailing '#' two positions ahead is what distinguishes a two-digit code from a single digit, so scanning with that lookahead decodes each letter unambiguously.
hints: The only thing that turns a one-digit code into a two-digit one is a '#' marker that follows two characters later.
Walk left to right: peek at the character two slots ahead - if it is '#', this is a two-digit code; otherwise it is a single digit.
Consume three characters (digit, digit, '#') for a marked code or one character for a plain digit, convert the number to its letter with chr(ord('a') + value - 1), and advance accordingly.

edge_inputs.description: mix of two-digit and single-digit codes
single digits then a hashed code at the end
single nine must not be mistaken for part of a hashed code
a hashed code immediately followed by a bare digit that looks like it could pair
only single digits one through nine
consecutive hashed codes spanning the alphabet tail

stress.description: long random string of valid codes near the length cap
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    out = []
    length = 0
    cap = max(1, size)
    while length < cap:
        if rng.random() < 0.5 and length + 3 <= cap:
            v = rng.randint(10, 26)
            out.append(str(v) + '#')
            length += 3
        else:
            v = rng.randint(1, 9)
            out.append(str(v))
            length += 1
    return [''.join(out)]
stress.note: constructs only valid encodings so the mapping is always defined
